stories.headline: DIA audit finds overbilling errors, uncollected penalties worth millions from airlines
stories.summary:
Denver Auditor Timothy O'Brien released a report Thursday finding that Denver International Airport, which collects more than $570 million annually in airline revenue, has made billing errors resulting in one airline being overcharged by nearly $6 million. The audit also found that the airport failed to collect millions in penalties owed by carriers including United and Frontier Airlines.

The findings raise questions about the financial management practices at one of the nation's busiest airports. Airport officials have not publicly responded to the audit's specific findings as of Thursday.

The report comes as DIA is also preparing for approximately 8 million passengers in July and is undergoing infrastructure modernization upgrades. A separate $43 million investment in airport infrastructure is also in the pipeline.

stories.headline: Denver police release body cam footage from June 2 shooting; object believed to be gun was actually a lighter
stories.summary:
Denver police on Thursday released body-worn camera footage from a June 2 shooting involving a Denver Police officer, an Adams County Sheriff's deputy and a Colorado State Patrol trooper at a Sinclair gas station near the intersection of 6th Avenue and Santa Fe Drive. Officers had pursued a suspect driving a stolen traffic control vehicle and shot at him after a camera inside the stolen truck showed him holding what appeared to be a firearm.

DPD Major Crimes Division Commander Matt Clark said at a news conference Thursday that the investigation revealed the object was actually a lighter designed to look like a handgun. Officers said they believed the man was armed based on what they observed through the truck's interior camera footage prior to the confrontation.

The release of the footage is part of DPD's standard protocol for officer-involved shootings. The incident is still under investigation.

stories.headline: Three Colorado motorcyclists killed in Idaho crash; Colorado driver arrested
stories.summary:
Three Colorado motorcyclists died Tuesday, June 16, on U.S. Highway 12 in northern Idaho after a pickup truck driver, also from Colorado, struck them, according to Idaho State Police. The crash prompted an arrest of the pickup truck driver, though further details on charges were not immediately available.

Idaho State Police responded to the fatal multi-vehicle collision and are leading the investigation. The identities of the victims had not been fully released as of Thursday.

The incident is a significant loss for Colorado's riding community and highlights the dangers of long-distance motorcycle travel. Authorities have not released the name of the driver arrested in connection with the crash.

stories.headline: Wildfires prompt evacuations on Colorado's Western Slope and in Fremont County
stories.summary:
An intense wildfire on Redlands Mesa near the intersection of North Road and Rimrock forced evacuations Thursday in the Hotchkiss area of the Western Slope, according to the Hotchkiss Fire District. Officials said the blaze was too intense for ground crews, requiring aerial resources.

Separately, an evacuation notice was issued to residents along County Road F45 in Fremont County on the morning of June 18 due to a wildfire near State Highway 115. Fire crews were working to control that blaze as of a mid-morning update.

The fires come during an extended period of high fire danger across Colorado, with dry conditions, elevated temperatures and critical fire weather affecting much of the state. Residents in affected areas were urged to monitor official communications and be ready to leave on short notice.

stories.headline: Colorado skydiving instructor from Grand Junction among 12 killed in Missouri plane crash
stories.summary:
Jennifer L. Sharp, 55, of Grand Junction, a veteran skydiving instructor with more than 6,800 jumps, was among the 12 people killed when a plane crashed shortly after takeoff in Butler, Missouri, on Sunday. Sharp had been coaching 25-year-old Blake Thacker toward his skydiving certification that weekend. Friends and colleagues described her as one of the most accomplished instructors in the sport.

The Pacific Aerospace 750XL turboprop, operated by Skydive Kansas City, was only about 100 feet in the air when it made an abrupt left turn and crashed on a sunny day, witnesses said. The plane had arrived at the Butler airport for the first time on June 5 and still bore advertising from a Tennessee skydiving company.

The National Transportation Safety Board is investigating all factors contributing to the crash, including the pilot's experience with the aircraft model and any mechanical or structural issues. Sharp's friends recalled her jumping into Coors Field dressed as the Queen of England and her passion for sharing the sport with first-time jumpers.

stories.headline: Denver's Juneteenth music festival expands to three-day celebration in Five Points
stories.summary:
Denver's annual Juneteenth celebration returns to the historic Five Points neighborhood with an expanded three-day format this year, featuring a parade, street fair with hundreds of vendors and food trucks, and live music performances on Welton Street between 24th and 29th streets. The parade begins at Manual High School at 11 a.m. Saturday, June 20.

Headlining the main stage is Grammy-nominated R&B artist SiR, joined by regional and national acts performing hip hop, soul and R&B. Organizers say the expansion reflects growing community interest in the annual commemoration of the end of slavery in the United States.

The Five Points neighborhood has historically been the cultural heart of Denver's Black community, making it a meaningful backdrop for the celebration. More information is available at juneteenthmusicfestival.com.

stories.headline: Arizona man rolls SUV down Imogene Pass after hitting ice on closed Colorado route
stories.summary:
A 58-year-old Phoenix man rolled his Toyota SUV down a mountainside on Imogene Pass Thursday morning after striking an icy patch a few switchbacks below the 13,114-foot summit, San Miguel County sheriff's officials said. The man was able to exit the overturned vehicle, walk back up to the unpaved road and was transported to a hospital with non-life-threatening injuries.

The driver had entered the pass from the Ouray County side, unaware that the San Miguel County side remained under its winter closure because no signs had been posted on the road. Ouray County road crews later posted signs near the summit warning that the San Miguel side is closed.

Imogene Pass is a rugged former mining road requiring off-road vehicles with low-range gearing, four-wheel drive and high clearance. The pass connects Ouray and Telluride and gains nearly 5,000 feet of elevation over its 17.9-mile length.

stories.headline: Two Denver bars named finalists for best new cocktail bar in the U.S. at Spirited Awards
stories.summary:
The Peach Crease Club in RiNo and Rougarou in Five Points are two of four finalists nationwide for the title of Best New U.S. Cocktail Bar at the 20th annual Spirited Awards, giving Denver a 50 percent chance of claiming the top honor. The other two finalists are schmuck. in New York City and Vandell in Los Angeles. Winners will be announced at a ceremony in New Orleans on July 23.

The Peach Crease Club, which opened last November near Mission Ballroom, is owned by local cocktail figures Alex Jump and Stuart Jensen and features culinary-inspired drinks in an audiophile-friendly setting. Rougarou debuted last August from the team behind Yacht Club — itself a 2024 Spirited Award winner — and is conceived as a tribute to the American South.

Denver's recognition reflects the city's growing national reputation in the cocktail industry. The Spirited Awards are part of the annual Tales of the Cocktail conference, which gathers leading mixologists from around the world each summer.

stories.headline: SRM Concrete files plans for new North Denver facility as Broncos stadium site speculation continues
stories.summary:
Tennessee-based SRM Concrete has submitted concept plans to Denver for a new wet batch plant at 5150 N. Brighton Blvd., a 4.92-acre site north of Denver. The plans, filed by Missouri engineering firm Premier Design Group, call for demolishing two existing buildings and constructing a new office building, truck and employee parking, a drying pit and other infrastructure.

The development is notable because SRM's existing plant at 1145 Quivas St. near Burnham Yard sits on land considered a key piece of the Denver Broncos' proposed new stadium and entertainment district. Earlier this year, SRM CEO Jeff Hollingshead stated there is "no transaction or agreement in place" to sell the Quivas Street location.

The new Brighton Boulevard facility, if approved, could give SRM additional operational capacity independent of any future Burnham Yard redevelopment. Neither SRM nor Premier Design Group responded to requests for comment by deadline.

stories.headline: Broncos offseason program wraps with seven key takeaways, including Waddle's impact and backup QB battle
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos closed out their offseason program after head coach Sean Payton canceled the final day of mandatory minicamp Thursday, telling reporters the staff has seen enough before players return in late July for training camp in pads. New play-caller Davis Webb ran an offense that showed early success attacking defenses in the flat and short-to-intermediate middle of the field during OTA and minicamp practices.

New receiver Jaylen Waddle, acquired in the offseason, quickly emerged as a featured weapon, with Denver Post observers noting his yards-after-catch ability exceeded that of any Broncos wideout not named Troy Franklin last season. Quarterback Bo Nix acknowledged Waddle's speed as irreplaceable.

A potential backup quarterback competition and a cornerback battle between second-year player Ja'Quan McMillian and Riley Moss are among the storylines heading into training camp. Second-year cornerback Jaylon Barron is also pushing for a starting role, with financial implications for Denver's future roster decisions involving both McMillian and Moss.

stories.headline: Metro Denver inflation hits 5% in May, highest rate since 2023, driven by energy costs
stories.summary:
Metro Denver's inflation rate reached 5 percent in May 2026, the highest level since 2023, with energy costs identified as the primary driver of the increase. The figure outpaces national inflation trends and reflects pressures that have persisted across the region's economy.

Rising energy prices have rippled through housing, transportation and consumer goods costs for Denver-area residents. The uptick comes as Colorado cities are also contending with escalating expenses related to new water infrastructure and high housing costs.

Economists and local officials are monitoring the trend heading into summer, when energy demand typically increases. The data adds pressure on households already dealing with elevated costs across multiple spending categories.

stories.headline: Water World in Federal Heights to open its largest expansion since 1986 with new Summit Canyon area
stories.summary:
Water World in Federal Heights is set to open its Summit Canyon area, the largest new development at the popular water park since 1986. The expansion adds significant new attractions to the facility, which draws visitors from across the Denver metro area each summer.

The announcement comes as Colorado heads into a hot summer season, with near-record temperatures already recorded in Denver and high demand expected at water recreation facilities. Water World has been a regional draw for decades and the new development is expected to increase attendance.

Details on the specific rides and opening date for the Summit Canyon area were not fully disclosed in available reporting. The park is located at 88th Avenue and Pecos Street in Federal Heights.

joke.joke: My phone autocorrected 'love you' to 'love yogurt.' I sent it anyway. The message was received well.

weekendEvents.title: Juneteenth Music Festival – Five Points
weekendEvents.description: One of the nation's largest Juneteenth celebrations returns to Denver's historic Five Points neighborhood with music performances, vendors, and the city's longest-running parade. Friday night's Southern Soul Party in The Plaza features Denver DJ Suga Bear; the rest of the weekend's events are free.
weekendEvents.time: Fri. 7–11 p.m.; Sat. noon–8 p.m.; Sun. 2 p.m.–midnight
weekendEvents.location: Five Points Plaza and multiple locations, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Louis Tomlinson at Red Rocks Amphitheatre
weekendEvents.description: Former One Direction member Louis Tomlinson brings his solo tour to Red Rocks, performing Britpop-inspired rock and stadium anthems. Indie-pop act The Aces opens the evening.

weekendEvents.title: Iration at Red Rocks Amphitheatre
weekendEvents.description: Reggae and rock outfit Iration performs a sunset set at Red Rocks as part of Denver's packed summer concert weekend.

weekendEvents.title: Gorgon City and SG Lewis at The Junkyard
weekendEvents.description: UK dance music duo Gorgon City headlines alongside SG Lewis, Jackie Hollander, and Denver's own Option4 for one of the weekend's biggest electronic dance events.

weekendEvents.title: Emancipator at Mission Ballroom
weekendEvents.description: Electronic artist Emancipator brings his signature blend of downtempo and live instrumentation to Mission Ballroom for one of the weekend's most anticipated indoor performances.

weekendEvents.title: Brilliant RiNo – Summer Solstice Celebration
weekendEvents.description: A free outdoor event in the RiNo Art District featuring illuminated circus performances, light projections, aura photography, and live music to mark the summer solstice.

weekendEvents.title: 1940s Summer Ball at Boulder Municipal Airport
weekendEvents.description: Guests are invited to dress in 1940s attire and swing dance to the Glenn Miller Orchestra under the stars, with World War II-era planes and vintage cars on display.
weekendEvents.location: Boulder Municipal Airport, 3393 Airport Road, Boulder

weekendEvents.title: African Creators Festival
weekendEvents.description: The fourth annual African Creators Festival comes to the Cookie Factory for an evening of food, music, and cultural storytelling celebrating African artists and creators across the continent and diaspora.
weekendEvents.location: Cookie Factory, 425 W. Fourth Ave., Denver

weekendEvents.title: Highlands Street Fair
weekendEvents.description: An annual neighborhood street fair in the Highlands featuring local vendors, food, and community entertainment.

weekendEvents.title: Arts Thornton Music Series: Inside the Orchestra
weekendEvents.description: A 45-minute interactive family concert at Trail Winds Recreation Center celebrating Juneteenth through the works of three Black composers, designed for children 10 and under. Kids can meet musicians and explore instruments after the performance.
weekendEvents.location: Trail Winds Recreation Center, 13495 Holly St., Thornton

weekendEvents.title: For The Culture Cookout – Juneteenth at Cheesman Park
weekendEvents.description: A free Juneteenth gathering at Cheesman Park rooted in celebration, connection, and community unity.

weekendEvents.title: Harry Potter and the Cursed Child
weekendEvents.description: The Broadway production of Harry Potter and the Cursed Child, winner of six Tony Awards including Best Play, is in its final weekend of its Denver engagement.

topStory.headline: Switzerland Talks Collapse as Israeli Strikes Kill 18 in Lebanon, Hezbollah Kills Four Israeli Soldiers
topStory.summary:
Planned technical negotiations between the United States and Iran in the Swiss village of Obbürgen were abruptly cancelled Friday after Hezbollah killed four Israeli soldiers and Israel launched a wave of retaliatory airstrikes across southern Lebanon and the Bekaa Valley that left at least 18 people dead and 33 wounded. The cancellation came so suddenly that Vice President JD Vance's staff and a contingent of journalists had already assembled at Joint Base Andrews outside Washington in anticipation of the trip before the White House announced Vance would not be departing.

The talks had been scheduled just two days after the U.S. and Iran signed a 14-point memorandum of understanding that opened a 60-day window to negotiate a permanent agreement over Iran's nuclear program and restore oil traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. Tehran reportedly refused to send its delegation to Switzerland in response to Israel's continued military operations in Lebanon, which Iran has repeatedly identified as a non-negotiable precondition for any broader peace settlement. The White House said it looked forward to beginning technical talks "as soon as possible" but gave no revised timeline.

The surge in violence centered on the area around Nabatieh in southern Lebanon, where Israeli forces have maintained positions extending roughly 10 kilometers into Lebanese territory since before the ceasefire was announced. Hezbollah said its fighters targeted Israeli troops attempting to advance toward Nabatieh with rockets, drones, and artillery, while Israel's military said it struck more than 80 Hezbollah targets overnight in response to what it described as ceasefire violations. Four Israeli soldiers, including a battalion commander, were killed in the fighting.

The deaths of the Israeli soldiers prompted sharp statements from far-right members of Israel's government. National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir called for scorched earth tactics, stating "All of Lebanon must burn," while Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich called for Israel to "open the gates of hell." French Foreign Minister Jean-Noël Barrot responded by calling on Israel to halt its strikes and urging the United States to apply pressure on the Israeli government to honor the ceasefire agreement.

The 14-point MoU, whose text was read aloud by a U.S. official on a call with reporters but has not been released in physical form, calls for the immediate and permanent termination of military operations on all fronts including Lebanon and commits both sides to respecting Lebanese sovereignty. However, the agreement does not mention Israel, which is not a signatory and has stated it will retain military positions in Lebanon, Syria, and Gaza for an indefinite period. Israel's published maps of its current positions in Lebanon also appear to extend into Lebanese maritime territory, potentially violating a 2022 bilateral maritime agreement, according to legal experts.
